segments.s390-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a time clause. خورشید is the subject 'the sun'. بر چرخ is a prepositional phrase 'on the sky/heavens' (چرخ = sky here). بنمود is an archaic/poetic past tense of نمودن meaning 'showed/appeared'. چهر is the direct object 'face/appearance' that the sun 'showed' — word order is standard for Persian narrative: temporal conj. + subject + locative + verb + object.
segments.s390-g10: بیاراست is a past tense verb meaning 'adorned' (بیاراست = بیاراستن). روی زمین را is the direct object phrase 'the surface/face of the earth' with را marking definiteness/accusative; روی here means 'surface'. به مهر is a manner/instrumental phrase 'with/by love' (به used here = 'by/with'), showing how the earth was adorned.
segments.s390-g11: به برج حمل is a locative/temporal phrase 'in/at the sign/house of Aries' (به + noun phrase). برج here means the zodiac sign (not a literal tower), and حمل is the name of that sign (Aries). تاج بر سر نهاد is a verb phrase where نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put', taking تاج as object and بر سر 'upon (his) head' as the locative complement.
segments.s390-g12: از او begins a causal/agentive phrase 'from him / because of him'. خاور و باختر گشت شاد is a clause where گشت (past of گشتن) = 'became' and شاد = 'happy/joyful'; the subject (خاور و باختر, 'east and west') follows از او to show that east and west became glad on his account.
segments.s390-g13: پر از ... شد is a common predicative pattern meaning 'became full of ...' (پر = 'full (of)'). از introduces the contents 'غلغل و رعد' ('clamor and thunder'). شد is the past copular/auxiliary meaning 'became', and کوهسار is the subject 'mountain-range' placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s390-g14: Same structure as the previous segment: پر از + nouns + شد + subject. Here 'پر از نرگس و لاله شد جویبار' means 'the stream became full of narcissi and tulips' — جویبار (stream) is the subject, شد = 'became', and نرگس / لاله are the items filling it.
segments.s390-g15: ز (poetic variant of از) = 'from'. This clause lists effects attributed to flowers: 'ز لاله فریب' = 'from the tulip (came) seduction/enticement' where فریب is a noun meaning 'enticement/allure'. The conjunction و links the next parallel clause 'ز نرگس نهیب' = 'and from the narcissus (came) a rebuke/thunderous cry' — نهیب here functions as a noun meaning a loud rebuke/cry.
segments.s390-g16: Same pattern continues: ز سنبل عتیب = 'from the hyacinth (came) reproach' where عتیب is 'reproach' (noun). و ز گلنار زیب = 'and from the pomegranate-blossom (came) beauty' with زیب = 'beauty/adornment'. The short verbless clauses are elliptic; the verb 'came' or 'was' is implied and supplied by context.
segments.s390-g1: کنون is an adverb ‘now’. ز این سپس is a poetic prepositional/time phrase meaning ‘after this’ or ‘from this onward’ (ز + این + سپس), placed before the direct object هفتخوان (‘the seven trials’). آورم is first-person present/subjunctive used here to express the poet’s intention (‘I will bring/relate’); in classical Persian the present-subjunctive often conveys future or prospective action.
segments.s390-g2: سخنهای is the plural noun ‘sayings/speeches/utterances’ (with plural suffix -های). نغز و جوان are two coordinating adjectives modifying it (‘choice/pithy (naghz) and fresh/vigorous (javan)’), joined by و. The verb آورم repeats and closes the clause, giving a parallel clause structure: ‘I will bring/relate (these) pithy and fresh sayings.’
segments.s390-g3: اگر introduces a conditional protasis (‘if’). بخت is the subject ‘fortune/luck’, یکباره is an adverb ‘suddenly/at once’, یاری is the object ‘help’, and کند is the present/subjunctive third-person singular of کردن used to mean ‘gives/does’ (here: ‘should give help’). The whole clause is a standard Persian ‘if …’ conditional.
segments.s390-g4: بر او is a prepositional phrase ‘toward/to him’ (بر marking direction/beneficiary). طبع من is a noun + possessive (‘my temperament/poetic bent’ — طبع followed by the pronoun من as postposed possessor). کامگاری is the result noun ‘success/fulfillment’, and کند is the verb again (‘may/might bring about’); together the phrase means ‘may my nature attain success toward him.’
segments.s390-g5: بگویم is first-person present/subjunctive ‘I will say / I shall tell’ (verb placed before the adjunct). به تأیید محمود شاه is a prepositional/instrumental phrase ‘by/with the approval of Mahmud Shah’ (به + تأیید = ‘with/by the approval of’), specifying that what follows is told with the king’s sanction.
segments.s390-g6: بدان here functions as a directional/identifying unit meaning ‘to that’ / ‘of that’ (poetic contraction of به + آن) and introduces فر (‘farr’, the royal radiance/glory) as its object. و connects the coordinated phrase آن خسروانی کلاه ‘that royal crown(s)’. کلاه (literally ‘hat’) is used as a royal emblem (crown).
segments.s390-g7: که here introduces a wishing/optative clause (‘that’ / ‘may it be that’). شاه جهان is the noun phrase ‘the king of the world’, جاودان is the adjective ‘eternal’, and زنده‌باد is an optative formula meaning ‘may he live’ or ‘long live’. The order places the optative verb at the end, as is common in Persian benedictions.
segments.s390-g8: بزرگان گیتی is a genitive noun phrase ‘the great ones of the world’. ورا is an object/beneficiary pronoun ‘to him’ (i.e., ‘for him’). بنده is the predicate nominal ‘servant’, and باد is the optative particle here meaning ‘may’ (wishing particle). Together: ‘may the great men of the world be his servants.’
segments.s390-g25: به خنده is an adverbial phrase of manner ('with a laugh'), where به is used adverbially ('by/with'). بدو (to him) is the indirect object placed before the verb, and گوید is the 3rd-person present/past-form verb 'he says' (the subject is omitted). ای شوخ چشم is a vocative (direct address) following the verb; شوخ modifies چشم and forms a fixed epithet 'playful-eyed'.
segments.s390-g26: This is the quoted/exhortative predicate: گریـان is a present participial/predicative form meaning 'weeping' used as an exhortation ('be weeping' / 'weep'). به عشق تو gives the cause/target ('for/by your love' — هدف/سبب), with به functioning as 'for/of'. نه introduces the negated cause that follows (نه از درد و خشم = 'not from pain and anger'); از marks the causal source ('from/because of').
segments.s390-g27: نخندد is the verb with the negative prefix ن- attached to the present stem (3rd-person form) meaning 'does not / will not laugh.' زمین is the subject and تا introduces the subordinate temporal clause ('until') whose verb is نگرید (3rd-person present: 'weeps'). The word order is inverted (verb before subject in the main clause and verb before subject in the subordinate clause) as is common in poetry.
segments.s390-g28: هوا را is the direct object marked by را. نخوانم is 1st-person singular negative ('I do not / I will not call/name'); here خواندن carries the sense 'to call/name' rather than 'to read.' کف پادشا is the predicate nominal (no copula): کف = 'applause/palm' and پادشا = 'the king', so the speaker says he will not call the sky the king's applause/palm.
segments.s390-g29: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause ('for/that'). باران او is a nominal phrase ('his rain' — a figurative generosity/benefit), در بهاران uses در + season to mean 'in spring,' and بود is the 3rd-person past copula 'was.' The clause states a past characteristic (his 'rain' occurred in spring).
segments.s390-g30: نه ... چون is a negated comparison ('not like'), with چون introducing the standard of comparison. همت شهریاران is the noun phrase 'the ambition/resolve of kings' (شهریاران plural), and بود is again the past copula 'was.' The whole line contrasts the subject's 'rain' with the ambitions of rulers.
segments.s390-g31: به خورشید ماند is a verbal phrase meaning 'resembles the sun' (ماندن with به + noun expresses likeness); همی is a frequent classical adverb meaning 'ever' or 'always.' دست شاه is the subject phrase 'the king's hand' (positioned after the verb for poetic inversion). Overall: 'the king's hand always resembles the sun.'
segments.s390-g32: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause ('when'); اندر حمل is a genitive/adverbial phrase 'in (the act of) carrying/holding.' برفرازد is 3rd-person singular present/future with the intensive/exaltive prefix (بر-) meaning 'he lifts up / exalts,' and کلاه is the object 'the hat/crown.' The clause describes the action that makes the hand 'like the sun.'
segments.s390-g41: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'). جنگ آیدش is literally 'battle comes to him' (آید + enclitic ش = 'comes to him'); in epic Persian آید here sets the time/background (‘when battle came upon him’). پیش جَنگ آورد is the main clause: آورد (bring) takes جنگ as its object and پیش functions adverbially ('forward/before'), so the clause means 'he brought/advanced the battle forward' (i.e. pressed into combat). Word order is poetic: temporal clause + verb phrase with object before the verb.
segments.s390-g42: سر شهریاران is an object phrase 'the heads of the chiefs' (the genitive relation is implicit without an ezāfe). به چنگ آورد is an idiomatic verb phrase 'to bring into the claw/hand' = 'to seize'; به marks direction/into and آورد is third-person singular past/present-historical 'he brought/seized.' The clause states the violent result of the previous line: he seized the leaders' heads.
segments.s390-g43: بدان کس is from به + آن 'to that person' (poetic contraction) and introduces the beneficiary. که introduces a relative clause 'who...'. گردن نهد literally 'lays down/sets his neck' (نهد = to place/put) — an idiom for submission/surrender. گنج خویش = 'his treasure' (خویش = 'his own' formed here from خوی + ش). The verb that follows supplies the action toward that beneficiary.
segments.s390-g44: ببخشد is a third-person form meaning 'he gives/bestows' (ببخشد here = 'gives generously'). نیندیشد is a negated present/habitual 'does not think' (he does not consider/hesitate). از رنج خویش = 'of/from his suffering' — a prepositional phrase indicating what he does not think about. The two verbs form a coordinated statement about giving and lack of concern for one's own hardship.
segments.s390-g45: جهان را is a topicalized/affected phrase 'as for the world' (را marks the definite/theme). جهاندار is the predicate noun 'ruler/sovereign' and محمود is an epithet/appositive ('praised' or 'Mahmud' used poetically). باد is the optative particle 'may (it be)'. The whole segment is a wish: 'May the world's ruler be Mahmoud/praiseworthy.'
segments.s390-g46: از او marks source 'from him'. بخشش و داد are the nouns 'generosity/grant' and 'justice' coordinated by و. موجود باد repeats the optative 'may ... exist/be present.' Together: 'May generosity and justice proceed/exist from him.' Note باد again marks a wish.
segments.s390-g47: ز رویین دژ is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the iron fortress' (ز = from, poetic). اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. جهاندیده پیر is a compound noun phrase 'the world‑experienced (i.e. worldly-wise) old man.' Together they set the scene/time and identify the person to be observed.
segments.s390-g48: نگر is an imperative 'look!' followed by a purpose/result clause introduced by تا 'so that / in order that' (here 'so that' / 'to see'). چه گوید = 'what he says' (چه = what; گوید = 'he says' — the listed senses for گوید do not include 'say' so see senses note). از او یاد گیر is another imperative 'take/receive memory (i.e. learn) from him' — از = from, او = him, یاد گیر literally 'take memory' = 'learn; take note'.
segments.s390-g33: If-clause: اگر is the conditional 'if'. گنج is the noun 'treasure'. پیش آید is a light verb phrase 'comes forth / appears' (آید = 'comes'); پیش here participates in the verbal phrase 'پیش آید' meaning 'appear'. از introduces the source 'from/out of' and خاک خشک = 'dry earth/soil' (خاک = earth, خشک = dry). Word order is flexible/classical: the verb phrase follows the subject and the source phrase follows it.
segments.s390-g34: Continuation of the conditional alternatives: وگر = 'or if / but if' introduces another possibility; آب دریا is a genitival noun phrase 'water of the sea' (آب = water, دریا = sea). The second وگر repeats to introduce a further alternative; در here is being used as a noun (classical Persian reading 'dorr' = pearl/jewel) and مشک = 'musk'. The main verb آید is understood from the previous clause and is omitted (ellipsis across the coordinated alternatives).
segments.s390-g35: ندارد (lit. 'does not have') is the negative present verb 'does not possess'; همی is a classical adverb meaning 'ever/always' or emphatic present; روشناییش is the possessed noun 'its brightness' (روشنایی + -ش); باز is an adverb here 'again' or 'back'. Together they mean 'it never regains its brightness' or 'its brightness does not return.' Note that classical word order allows the verb to appear before its object.
segments.s390-g36: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. درویش = 'dervish/ascetic' (here a person who might receive alms). The token shown as وز should be read as و + ز ('and from'), joining the second source; شاه گردن‌فراز = 'the proud-necked king' (شاه = king, گردن‌فراز an attributive adjective 'proud' literally 'neck-high/ proud-necked'). The phrase thus lists two sources ('from the dervish and from the proud king').
segments.s390-g37: کف = 'palm (of the hand)'. The following noun phrase شاه ابوالقاسم is a possessive/genitive construction 'the palm of King Abūl-Qāsem'. آن پادشا is an appositive 'that king/sovereign' referring back to the named monarch. Classical Persian often strings nouns and titles in this way without overt particles.
segments.s390-g38: چنین است is the demonstrative copular phrase 'is thus' or 'is so' (چنین = thus, است = is). با here marks attitude/treatment 'toward/with' and is followed by two coordinated adjectives/nouns پاک و ناپارسا = 'the pure and the impious' (پاک = pure/clean, ناپارسا = impious/unholy). The clause states how the king behaves toward both kinds.
segments.s390-g39: دریغش = 'his/its reluctance' (دریغ + possessive -ش). نیاید is a negative impersonal/present form 'does not come/appear', used idiomatically as 'he feels no reluctance' (lit. 'reluctance does not come to him'). ز introduces the source/reason 'from/regarding', بخشیدن is the infinitive 'giving/granting', and ایچ (classical form of هیچ) means 'anything/nothing' used here as 'not a thing' — together 'he has no reluctance about giving anything.'
segments.s390-g40: نه is the negator 'not'; آرام here is a noun/adverb 'rest/peace'; گیرد (گیرد) is the present-subjunctive/indicative form of گرفتن used in the sense 'takes/gets' — so آرام گیرد = 'take rest'. به‌روز is a temporal phrase 'by day / in the day' (classical usage 'of the day'), and بسیچ (بسیچ/بسیار) = 'very/much/many'. The clause means 'he does not take rest by the day much' i.e. 'he does not rest (even over many days)'.
segments.s390-g17: پر here is the poetic adjective 'full (of)' placed before the following noun phrase; آتشِ دل (literally 'fire of the heart') means passionate fire or ardor modifying ابر ('cloud'), so the phrase reads 'a cloud whose heart is full of fire'. The conjunction و links a parallel phrase پر آب چشم, where پر again means 'full (of)' and آب چشم = 'water of the eye' i.e. tearful/teary eyes.
segments.s390-g18: خروش مغانی is a nominal phrase 'the clamor/cry/noise of the magāni' — here مغانی functions as 'songs' or 'singers' producing sound. The following و links a second descriptive unit: پر تاب خشم, where پر = 'full (of)' and تاب is used in the sense of radiance/force or sheen; خشم = 'anger' — together 'full of the radiance/force of anger'.
segments.s390-g19: چو ('when') introduces a temporal clause. آتش نماید uses نماید (from نمودن) 'shows itself/appears' (3rd person). بپالاید is the verb 'purify' in the same verbal form (verb placed before its subject here, as is common in poetry): آب is the subject ('water purifies'). The structure means 'when fire appears, water purifies (it/that)'.
segments.s390-g20: ز = 'from' or 'by' (poetic). ز آواز او = 'at/by his voice'; سر برآید is a verbal phrase 'the head rises' (برآید = 'arises/appears'), an idiom for waking up; ز خواب = 'from sleep'. Together: 'at his voice the head rises from sleep' (i.e. his voice wakes someone).
segments.s390-g21: چو + verbal phrase expresses a temporal clause: چو بیدار گردی = 'when you become awake' (بیدار = awake; گردی here is the verbal form meaning 'you become/you turn'). جهان را ببین is an imperative/instructive clause 'see/observe the world' with را marking the definite object. The sentence addresses the addressee directly.
segments.s390-g22: که introduces an explanatory clause: 'for/that ...'. دیباست‌گر is the agent noun 'brocade‑maker' (the one who makes brocade); the clause is elliptical/conditional — 'were there a brocade‑weaver (to work on it)'. نقش مانی = 'Mani's design/pattern' (Mani being the famous painter/designer); به چین = 'into its folds' (به = into/toward, چین = fold/wrinkle). So the line says the world is like brocade in which a brocade‑weaver would set Mani's pattern into its folds.
segments.s390-g23: چو + predicate describes a time: 'when/as'. رخشنده گردد = 'becomes radiant/bright' (رخشنده = radiant, گردد = becomes). جهان is the subject and ز آفتاب is an ablative phrase 'from the sun/from sunlight' giving the cause of the radiance.
segments.s390-g24: رخ نرگس و لاله = 'the face of narcissus and (the face of) tulip'. بینی here is the 2nd‑person present 'you see' (not 'nose'); the verb follows its object as is common in poetry, so 'رخ نرگس و لاله بینی' = 'you see the faces of narcissus and tulip'. پر آب after the verb is a predicate/adjective phrase 'full of water' (i.e. tear‑brimming).

sentences.s390-s5: When the sun showed its face on the sky, it adorned the surface of the earth with love.
sentences.s390-s6: In the sign of Aries he set the crown upon his head; because of him east and west became glad.
sentences.s390-s7: The mountain-range became full of clamor and thunder; the stream became full of narcissi and tulips.
sentences.s390-s8: From the tulip came seduction, and from the narcissus a rebuke; from the hyacinth reproach, and from the pomegranate-blossom beauty.
sentences.s390-s1: Now, after this, I will bring the Seven Trials; I will relate witty, fresh sayings.
sentences.s390-s2: If luck should suddenly lend its aid, may my poetic bent find success with him.
sentences.s390-s3: I will say, with the approval of King Mahmud, of that farr and those royal crowns.
sentences.s390-s4: May the king of the world live forever; may the great ones of the earth be his servants.
sentences.s390-s13: He says to him with a laugh, "O playful-eyed one, be weeping for love of you, not from pain and anger."
sentences.s390-s14: The earth will not laugh until the sky weeps; I will not call the sky the king's applause.
sentences.s390-s15: For his rain was in spring, not like the ambition of kings.
sentences.s390-s16: The king's hand ever resembles the sun, when, while carrying, he raises the crown aloft.
sentences.s390-s21: When battle came upon him he pressed forward into the fighting, and he laid the chiefs' heads in his grip.
sentences.s390-s22: He gives his treasure to the man who lays down his neck; he does not think of his own suffering.
sentences.s390-s23: May the world's sovereign be the praised Mahmud; may generosity and justice be present from him.
sentences.s390-s24: Now look upon the iron fortress and the worldly-wise old man; see what he says and learn from him.
sentences.s390-s17: If treasure comes forth from dry earth, or if the water of the sea, or if pearls and musk (appear).
sentences.s390-s18: Its brightness does not return again, neither from the dervish nor from the proud-necked king.
sentences.s390-s19: The palm of King Abūl-Qāsem, that sovereign, is thus toward the pure and the impious.
sentences.s390-s20: He has no reluctance in granting anything; he takes no rest by day for long (i.e. he is unstinting and never rests).
sentences.s390-s9: A cloud with its heart on fire and its eyes full of water; the clamor of songs and the full radiance of wrath.
sentences.s390-s10: When the fire shows itself, the water purifies; at his voice the head rises from sleep.
sentences.s390-s11: When you awake, behold the world; it is like brocade — were there a brocade‑weaver, he would set Mani's pattern into its folds.
sentences.s390-s12: When the world grows radiant from the sun, behold the face of narcissus and the tulip — you will see them full of water.
